title: The Economic Cover-Up Behind America's Iran War
narrative: Eight weeks into the Iran war, the gap between what the Trump administration says about the economy and what Americans are actually experiencing has become a chasm. Gas above $4 a gallon. Inflation at 3.8 percent — the sharpest spike in nearly three years. Ten thousand jobs lost per month, according to Goldman Sachs. And the president himself, when asked if Americans' financial pain influences his push for peace, answered: "Not even a little bit." This is what a cover-up looks like in 2026: not
telemetry: Eight weeks into the Iran war, the gap between what the Trump administration says about the economy and what Americans are actually experiencing has become a chasm.
Inflation at 3.8 percent — the sharpest spike in nearly three years.
Ten thousand jobs lost per month, according to Goldman Sachs.

And the president himself, when asked if Americans' financial pain influences his push for peace, answered: "Not even a little bit."

This is what a cover-up looks like in 2026: not a single dramatic lie, but a sustained, multi-channel campaign to minimize, mischaracterize, and bury the economic costs of a war of choice.
analysis: The Official Narrative vs. Reality White House spokesperson Kush Desai offered the official line in April: "The American economy remains on a solid trajectory." The March jobs report showed "robust private sector job growth," while core inflation was "cooling" and prices for "beef, dairy, eggs, and prescription drugs actually declining." The data tells a different story. Energy Costs: Brent crude surged from $72.48 per barrel before the war to $112.57 at peak — a 55 percent increase. Average gasoline prices climbed from $2.98 to $4.06 per gallon. In California, pump prices crossed $5 a gallon in the second week of March. Jet fuel more than doubled. Inflation: The Consumer Price Index hit 3.3 percent in April — the highest since May 2024. The Personal Consumption Expenditures index, the Federal Reserve's preferred measure, is projected to reach 4 percent by year-end, double the Fed's target. The Washington Post put the number at 3.8 percent in May — the sharpest single-month increase in nearly three years. Jobs: Goldman Sachs estimates the oil price shock is costing the U.S. economy approximately 10,000 jobs per month through the end of 2026. The leisure and hospitality sector absorbs roughly 5,000 of those losses; retail trade, another 2,000. The unemployment rate is projected to rise to 4.6 percent by Q3 — a 0.2 percentage point increase, half attributable to the war's energy disruption. Growth: The IMF cut its global growth forecast from 3.4 percent in 2025 to 3.1 percent in 2026 — the weakest since 2020. The UK faces the sharpest downgrade among G7 nations, with growth cut by 0.5 points to 0.8 percent. Bloomberg Economics projects global growth slowing to 2.9 percent in its base case. These aren't "cooling" numbers. They're the opposite. The Data Trump Cites That Doesn't Exist The New York Times reported on May 14 that Trump has been citing inaccurate data to downplay the war's economic toll. verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The administration knows this. The traders who placed $580 million in oil futures 27 seconds before Trump's announcement knew it too.
